title: MagiQware Secures €575K ($658K USD) Pre-Seed Allocation to Optimize Quantum Magic State Factories via Reinforcement Learning - Quantum Computing Report
gist: TU Delft quantum spin-off MagiQware raised €575K ($658K) pre-seed to build reinforcement-learning software compilers that cut the physical-qubit overhead of magic state factories, having already demonstrated up to a 40% reduction in distillation circuit length without changing hardware.

insights.claim: MagiQware, a TU Delft deep-tech spin-off, closed a €575,000 ($658K USD) pre-seed round, expanded from an initial €500K close, to fund product development, compiler validation, and engineering hiring.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A sub-$1M pre-seed signals a very early, pre-revenue quantum software bet; watch for a follow-on seed as compiler validation milestones land.
insights.quote: MagiQware has finalized its pre-seed funding round, reaching a total of €575,000 ($658K USD) in investment capital
insights.locator: Delft University of Technology (TU Delft) deep-tech

insights.claim: The round was led by early-stage investment manager LUMO Labs via the TTT.AI programme, with co-investment from Graduate Ventures and Delft Enterprises B.V.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: University-linked and regional deep-tech investors (LUMO, Delft Enterprises) anchor the cap table, indicating a Netherlands quantum ecosystem play rather than a global VC syndicate.
insights.quote: Initially launching with a €500K close led by early-stage investment manager LUMO Labs via the TTT.AI programme

insights.claim: The capital is earmarked to accelerate product development, validate automated software compilers, and expand the technical engineering team.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Use-of-proceeds focused on compiler validation and hiring implies the technology is still pre-commercial; milestones to watch are third-party compiler validation.
insights.locator: The capital injection is allocated to accelerate

insights.claim: Executing non-Clifford logical gates in fault-tolerant quantum computing requires magic state factories, whose distillation process can consume up to 90% of a full-stack quantum computer's physical qubit and circuit footprint.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: If distillation eats ~90% of qubits, overhead reduction is the key cost lever for FTQC economics; any company shrinking it directly attacks the dominant hardware cost.
insights.quote: frequently consuming up to 90% of a full-stack quantum computer’s physical qubit and circuit footprint
insights.locator: The startup addresses the steep physical resource overhead

insights.claim: Magic state factories distill high-fidelity magic states by filtering physical noise, but this distillation is the massive bottleneck in fault-tolerant quantum computing rather than standard quantum error correction.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Frames the FTQC scaling problem as a distillation-overhead problem, not just an error-correction problem, pointing investors toward the compilation/software layer.
insights.quote: the distillation process acts as a massive bottleneck
insights.locator: These factories filter out physical noise

insights.claim: MagiQware builds optimization tools inside the quantum compilation and software stack, using reinforcement learning models and automated AI agents to dynamically discover and optimize magic state distillation circuit architectures.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A software/AI approach to quantum overhead is hardware-agnostic and capital-light versus qubit fabrication, making it a differentiated bet in the quantum stack.
insights.quote: By using automated AI agents to dynamically discover and optimize distillation circuit architectures

insights.claim: MagiQware's compilers have demonstrated up to a 40% reduction in circuit length for target magic state factories, lowering aggregate hardware overhead without modifying the physical hardware layer.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A claimed 40% circuit-length cut achieved purely in software is the core commercial pitch; independent validation of this figure is the make-or-break datapoint.
insights.quote: demonstrated up to a 40% reduction in circuit length for target factories

insights.claim: Because MagiQware's optimization sits in software and does not touch the physical hardware layer, its overhead reductions could apply across full-stack quantum system developers regardless of qubit technology.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Hardware-agnostic positioning implies a potential picks-and-shovels sell-to-everyone model across competing qubit modalities.
insights.quote: dropping the aggregate hardware overhead needed by full-stack system developers without modifying the physical hardware layer

insights.claim: MagiQware is led by CEO Arash Ahmadi (PhD) and CTO Shakeeb Majid, with Head of Device Sahar Hejazi (PhD) and Head of Theory Ali Moghaddam (PhD), a PhD-heavy founding team spun out of TU Delft.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A research-credentialed team from a leading quantum institution (TU Delft) is the main de-risking signal at this pre-revenue stage.
insights.locator: Led by CEO Arash Ahmadi, PhD and CTO Shakeeb Majid
